tweets.text:
The Supreme Court will rule on birthright citizenship tomorrow morning…

It’s not an exaggeration to say this could be the most important decision in our lifetimes.

Under the current interpretation of the 14th Amendment, a foreign citizen of another nation can cross our border illegally at 9 months pregnant, give birth to an American citizen, and then return home. Their child is now eligible for US benefits and can vote in our elections.

The Chinese can also pay to have surrogate children on US territorial islands in the Pacific, then immediately ship the babies to China to be raised under the CCP. Those children are now eligible for US benefits and can vote in our elections.

Both of these scenarios are happening. It’s simply wrong and not at all what the Amendment was intended for, which was former slaves and their children during Reconstruction.

If it stands, US citizenship means nothing.

June was pretty busy for people farming rewards↓

1️⃣ @TrueCurrentX is the best active for traders right now

They have 0% trading fees, RFQ execution, self-custody, iOS mobile support, and access to more than just crypto majors in one seamless venue.

Your trading activity earns Airdrop Points, and users unlock their own referral code after $10k in cumulative notional volume.

After that, referrers earn a 20% bonus on the Points generated by referred traders, while referred users still keep their full Points.

So honestly, this makes sense if you're already trading perps, because that same activity can build points and give you exposure to a new venue early.

2️⃣ @grvt_io is running Season 2.0 rewards

Trading volume gets the biggest share of weekly points, but there is also things like:

→ deposits
→ strategies
→ referrals

They also have the 250K Volume Challenge running in June, with the prize pool starting at 50,000 USDT and scaling up to 250,000 USDT.

3️⃣ @paradex is running Season 3 XP, with 4M XP distributed weekly

Trading, liquidity, and community activity generate XP, while referrals add another layer on top.

Referrers earn 10% of referees’ XP, and referred users get a 5% XP bonus on trading activity.

It’s not the newest opportunity, but the weekly distribution makes it easier to track than campaigns where nobody knows what the points even mean.

4️⃣ @Polymarket’s current reward angle is more about liquidity rewards

For the World Cup 2026 markets, makers can earn by posting resting limit orders, with rewards distributed daily.

This isn’t for everyone because market making is more involved than just placing a trade. But it’s a good example of where reward campaigns are going.

My take:

I genuinely think that reward farming is getting more serious because the best opportunities right now are the ones where normal product usage creates reward exposure.

Obviously, none of this is free money, but if you’re already active in these markets anyway, it makes sense to know which venues are rewarding the activity you were probably going to do regardless.

tweets.text:
Raw volume can often be a vanity metric.

It's easily manipulated and inflated by points farming, fee promos, and routing tricks.

Here's how the top wallets rank on 90d revenue across swaps and perps:

→ @BinanceWallet: $136.8M (estimated)
→ @phantom: $23.3M (measured)
→ @MetaMask: $7.9M (measured + fee-derived)
→ @TrustWallet: $3.5M (fee-derived)
→ @rabby_io: $1.9M (measured)
→ @Rainbowdotme: $190K (fee-derived)

Measured/Fee-derived = there are onchain, verifiable fees.
Estimated (in this context) = no fees, so the number is hypothetical.

The 90 revenue number for Binance has 2 important caveats:

→ 0 % fee promo
→ Concentrated audience (57% of volume comes from the top 1,000 addresses farming Alpha points)

So that revenue number is what they'd hypothetically earn if they were charging fees, on volume that's mostly farming. It's the same vanity problem, just one layer up.

I could've easily made a "Binance Wallet is the leader in wallet revenue", but IMO, context is even more important than raw numbers.

Data from @ax1vc.

tweets.text:
Strategy recently announced its BTC Monetization Program, a framework that authorizes the company to sell bitcoin to fund the preferred dividend bill and finance preferred buybacks.

Our STRC report traced how the obligation behaves under pressure. While bitcoin appreciates, common equity carries the preferred dividends. Once mNAV falls below 1x, that channel stops working. Strategy is there now.

The cost moves down the structure. Common holders absorbed it first, through the dilution that funded the preferred dividends. The reserve and bitcoin sales come next. Strategy already tested that in late May, selling 32 coins to cover STRC distributions. The new framework makes it routine.

Facing this, Strategy had three options available: repurchase STRC, sell bitcoin, or suspend the dividend. It reached for two, authorizing buybacks and bitcoin sales, and rejected the last by raising the dividend rather than cutting it.

Strategy holds roughly 17 months of coverage in cash, so abandoning STRC would not require selling bitcoin today. The decision to authorize sales signals the opposite, that the 12% dividend remains funded and the channel stays open.

To keep the credit stack credible, Strategy will spend the asset the stack was built to accumulate. The bet was always about surviving long enough to be right.

In addition to a 99th percentile reading in the liquidity nowcast, there has never been an instance of both the liquidity impulse + liquidity composite being above the 75th percentile together - this is a first (in ~10 years of data I have for both)

Impulse drives the composite on a longer-term time horizon - this is usually why there is a negative contemporaneous correlation between the two

It is significantly more probable that 'liquidity' is higher not lower into 2027 and through that year imo - and I expect that when the *impulse* tops out and rolls over we will see that reflected in growing risk appetite over the subsequent 12-18 months 

(You can run your own studies like these using the regime data at https://t.co/oljeEa7GpB)

The people FUDing $CARDS on my timeline:

- angry sidelinooors
- rage bait engagement farmers
- uninformed "reSeArcHeRs"
- midcurvers who still think this is a toy
- people who care more about insider unlocks and Hayes' OTC dumps than actual business fundamentals

These people will hate on anything that looks remotely successful on the timeline, simply because they are so broken and dead inside from all the rugs and scams this industry has produced over the last few years.

They did the exact same thing with Hyperliquid after its launch. Now it's everyone's darling lol.

I get that trust is broken in this industry. Years of rugs and scams have conditioned people to assume the worst about everything. And honestly, I can't blame them.

But there's a world of difference between a founder who launches a token to extract and disappear, and a team that has been in the trenches for years building something real. If you can't tell the difference anymore, that's your problem.

After years of working side by side with @TuomHolmberg, @JoeMunnsHK and the entire Collector team, I am willing to put my full reputation behind them. Hardworking, humble, obsessively vision driven. These guys aren't stopping until every collector on eBay and Goldin has moved onchain to get the experience they deserve.

You can either see it now or you can see it in 12 months when everyone will call it retrospectively obvious.

The only difference will be the price.

> I know I’m not a simulated algorithm. The simulated algorithm isn’t conscious (we can stipulate). I am.

My interpretation of FDT has always been that you're supposed to conceive of your conscious self as simultaneously operating on all instantiations of it - on both your physical arm picking up the box, and the virtual arm inside of the simulation. Your consciousness is not situated either in the physical you alone, or in the simulated you alone. It is situated in the entire equivalence class of instantiations that are running the algorithm that is "you".

thread_tweets.text: And in terms of real world applicability, clearly the capability to do that level of faithful simulation does not exist, but on the other hand, our algorithms are leaky - lots of people can tell intuitively when someone is being dishonest. The most important core insight is that the conscious actions of our physical body are far from the only channel through which our mind's internal strategizing acts on the real world, and as a direct result of this, many forms of "machiavellian" strategizing often end up greatly underperforming what simple game theory models would predict. For these kinds of reasons folk morality is already somewhere in between CDT and FDT, even if not consciously stated.

The World Cup games are really something else. Beyond the competition and the games themselves—which are fantastic—there’s this incredible element of how people from different countries interact with each other.
People bring their unique personalities and nationalities, and everyone just gets along with so much respect. Whether it’s the Scots bringing their spirit and bagpipes to the games, or the Japanese fans cleaning up the stadium after a match, it’s a beautiful reflection of what the United Nations—and all countries—should be.
It brings out a joy, laughter, and a warmth that gives us a real element of optimism. We should learn from this whole experience of what it's like to be together, and how we can be together.
#WorldCup #soccer #raydalio

Gavin Baker: “I think Anthropic is worth $3 trillion today.”

Gavin laid out the case on the most recent All-In Podcast:

– They will end 2026 with over $100B in revenue

– They will likely end 2028 with $200-$300B in revenue

– At that scale, Anthropic will be very profitable because compute will be inference-dominated

– Anthropic reportedly has 85% gross margins on inference

– The market has already absorbed Anthropic’s valuation, it will just shift from private to public

@Jason:

“I'm sorry, did you say Anthropic is worth $3 trillion?”

Gavin:

“Yeah, I think that is roughly where it would probably trade as a public company.”

Travis Kalanick:

“Wow. Oh man. Holy sh*t.”

Gavin:

“They're going to end this year with well over $100 billion (in revenue). So what's the '28 number? Is it $200 billion? Is it $300 billion?”

“It's probably not going to trade at 10 times that number, and it will be very profitable at that scale because it'll be inference-dominated and people are reporting they have 85% gross margins on inference.”

“But in terms of the market absorbing this, like the market's already absorbed it. It's just shifting from private to public.”

“And so in the scale of global capital markets, these seem like really big numbers, but you're just moving from the private markets to the public markets, which are even bigger.”

I could not believe my eyes when I read what former Kansas City Fed President Esther George had to say in an interview today to the effect that she wouldn’t even wait till September to start hiking rates again.  And here we have Kevin Warsh at the helm, who prides himself on being an Alan Greenspan disciple (may the Maestro rest in peace).  The question is – would Greenspan really be talking about a rate hike?  He followed market indicators, not flawed government data like the CPI or PCE deflator.

As in:

(i) The yield curve:  The 2s/10s curve has flattened nearly +20 basis points since late May to just +30 basis points; (ii) The dollar:  In just five months, the DXY has strengthened nearly +6%; (iii) Credit spreads:  CCC-BB rated spreads have widened out more than +30 basis points since the end of May and are approaching 800 basis points for the first time since Liberation Day in April 2025; (iv) Commodity prices: The CRB index has rolled over by -13% from the nearby May peak; (v) Gold:  Down more than -10% in just the past month and by -27% from the early-year highs.

tweets.full_text:
It's rare in DC for the fate of major legislation to be truly unknown, but that's where we stand with the Clarity Act right now.

As of today, there's no deal, but also no lack of effort or urgency to get there in the next few weeks. July is the final stretch: it's now or never.

The main blocker is ethics. There's no path to 60 votes in the Senate unless Dems get some sort of provision that restricts the President, and maybe his family, from doing business in crypto.

Then there are lingering policy issues that were raised, but not solved, during markup.

The challenge is that there likely won't be a clear "yes" without putting the bill on the floor for a vote, but it's hard to justify using limited floor time on a bill that might not pass.

We may be in a high noise, low signal period while the work gets done behind the scenes.

@blairlmarshall For the 119th Congress.

Summary:

- USD Reserve policy: they are now committing to having at least 1y worth of dividends in it. They are also promising that it won't be used for anything other than div payments.

- STRC dividend: basically there is no clear guidance anymore, they are not guaranteeing a dividend increase even if it trades below par.

- STRC + other prefs buyback program: they are announcing that they might do it, but haven't done anything yet => Signal that they could buy these back if their price goes low enough

- MSTR buyback program: they are announcing that they might do it, but haven't don't anything yet. => Signal that MSTR mNAV *could* be defended below some threshold

- They remind everyone that they could be selling BTC for all sort of purposes, but still only sold MSTR and not BTC last week even at depressed mNAV

- They sold $1.1bn worth of MSTR shares to replenish the USD reserve, which now covers almost 1.5 year worth of dividends
